sanskrit_devanagari:
काङ्क्षन्तः कर्मणां सिद्धिं यजन्त इह देवताः ।
क्षिप्रं हि मानुषे लोके सिद्धिर्भवति कर्मजा ॥ १२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
kāṅkṣantaḥ karmaṇāṁ siddhiṁ yajanta iha devatāḥ |
kṣipraṁ hi mānuṣe loke siddhir bhavati karma-jā || 12 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Men in this world desire success in fruitive activities, and therefore they worship the demigods. Quickly, of course, men get results from fruitive work in this world.
Men in this world desire success in fruitive activities, and therefore they worship the demigods. Quickly, of course, men get results from fruitive work in this world.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3129 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There is a great misconception about the gods or demigods of this material world, and men of less intelligence, although passing as great scholars, take these demigods to be various forms of the Supreme Lord.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.12 Kanksantah, longing for, praying for; siddim, fruition, fructification of the results; karmanam, of actions; yajante, they worship; iha, here, in this world; devatah, the gods, Indra, Fire and others- which accords with the Upanisadic text, ‘While he who worships another god thinking, “He is on…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-12-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as Krishna's acknowledgement that ritual worship of specific deities for specific results does produce those results, and does so quickly in the human world. The verse establishes the validity of karma-kāṇḍa (ritual action) at its own level while implicitly pointing beyond it.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.12 All men, desirous of the fruits of their actions, ‘sacrifice’, i.e., worship or propitiate Indra and other divinities only. But nobody worships Me abandoning attachment to fruits — Me, who am the Self of Indra and other divinities and the real enjoyer of all sacrifices.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-12-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as part of the path-equivalence claim of 4.11 made concrete: those who approach the gods seeking specific fruits do receive them. The verse honors the karma-mīmāṁsā tradition (Vedic ritualism) without denying that more comprehensive fruits await different approaches.

positions.rendering: success born of fruitive work / ritualistic activities
specific worldly results produced quickly by deity-directed ritual action
the empirical fact that karma-yajña does produce its specified fruits

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three commentators read the verse as acknowledging that ritual worship genuinely produces its named fruits. The quietly important word is karma-jā — born of action. The fruit is a real consequence of the action, but it is the fruit-of-action level only; the verse implicitly contrasts with deeper paths.

literal_meaning: Those desiring success of actions worship the gods here; quickly indeed in the human world is success born of action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna acknowledges that those seeking specific outcomes through ritual action do receive those outcomes — the gods (devatāḥ) reciprocate ritual offerings with worldly results, and these results come quickly in the human realm. The verse is empirical, not condemnatory: there is a real karma-pathway to specific fruits. The implicit contrast is with the deeper liberation-path described in 4.9-4.11.
